coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of mercuric ions by sole intrinsic dual-emitting gold nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yan
Lu, Liqiang
Tian, Xike
Li, Yong
Yang, Chao
Nie, Yulun
Zhou, Zhaoxin
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing based on intrinsic dual-emitting nanomaterials is very intriguing for their simplicity and efficacy. However, intrinsic dual-emitting gold nanocluster-based fluorescence ratiometry remains unexplored. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence assay for detection of mercuric ions (Hg2+) has been developed using sole intrinsic dual-emitting gold nanoclusters (DE-AuNCs). The DE-AuNCs, simply synthesized by reducing chloroauric acid with an appropriate amount of glutathione, had two distinct intrinsic emission bands peaked at 610 nm and 810 nm respectively. When exposed to Hg2+, the 610 nm emission of the DE-AuNCs was remarkably quenched serving as a reporter signal, while the 810 nm emission almost kept unchanged serving as a reference signal, and thus giving a ratiometric signal. This ratiometric method showed high sensitivity and selectivity for sensing Hg2+ with low LOD down to 0.19 ppb which is quite lower than the WHO drinking-water guideline for inorganic mercury, and was successfully applied to the detection of Hg2+ in environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emitting gold nanoclusters
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Hg2+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hg2+ sensing in real water samples
Ratiometric fluorescence response of DE-AuNCs to Hg2+ and the mechanism
Optimization of the ratiometric sensing system
Sensitivity of the ratiometric Hg2+ sensing
Selectivity of the DE-AuNCs for Hg2+ sensing
Analysis of Hg2+ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum (left) and fluorescence emission spectrum of DE-AuNCs (right); (b) FT-IR spectra of GSH and DE-AuNCs; (c) HRTEM image of DE-AuNCs and (d) the corresponding size distribution.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the DE-AuNCs in the absence and presence of 200 ppb Hg2+. (b) CD spectra of GSH, 610 nm-emitting AuNCs, 810 nm-emittingAuNCs, DE-AuNCs and DE-AuNCs with 200 ppb Hg2+. (c) Schematic illustration of the sensing mechanism of Hg2+ by DE-AuNCs.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of the DE-AuNCs at different pH values. (b) Time-dependent fluorescence response of the DE-AuNCs to 200 ppb Hg2+.

(a) Evolution of fluorescence spectra of the DE-AuNCs upon addition of different concentrations of Hg2+. The concentration of Hg2+ from the top to the bottom: 0, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200, 225, 250, 275, 300 ppb, respectively. (b) Linear fitting curve of I 610/I 810 vs concentration of Hg2+.
(a) Fluorescence responses of the DE-AuNCs with different individual ions of 5 × 10−5 M and Cu2+, Ag+, Pd2+, Hg2+ (5 × 10−6 M), and (b) the corresponding ratiometric signals.
Comparison of LOD of fluorescence-based probes for Hg2+ sensing.
Recovery of the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Tested by cold atomic absorption method.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of mercuric ions by sole intrinsic dual-emitting gold nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Material Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Material Science and Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence assay for detection of mercuric ions (Hg2+) has been developed using sole intrinsic dual-emitting gold nanoclusters (DE-AuNCs). The addition of Hg2+ quenched the 610 nm emission, and the 810 nm emission remained unchanged.

Ratiometric fluorescence sensing based on intrinsic dual-emitting nanomaterials is very intriguing for their simplicity and efficacy. However, intrinsic dual-emitting gold nanocluster-based fluorescence ratiometry remains unexplored. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence assay for detection of mercuric ions (Hg2+) has been developed using sole intrinsic dual-emitting gold nanoclusters (DE-AuNCs). The DE-AuNCs, simply synthesized by reducing chloroauric acid with an appropriate amount of glutathione, had two distinct intrinsic emission bands peaked at 610 nm and 810 nm respectively. When exposed to Hg2+, the 610 nm emission of the DE-AuNCs was remarkably quenched serving as a reporter signal, while the 810 nm emission almost kept unchanged serving as a reference signal, and thus giving a ratiometric signal. This ratiometric method showed high sensitivity and selectivity for sensing Hg2+ with low LOD down to 0.19 ppb which is quite lower than the WHO drinking-water guideline for inorganic mercury, and was successfully applied to the detection of Hg2+ in environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric fluorescenceassay has attracted particular interest in recent years due to its inherent advantage of self-calibration for environmental effects over single emission fluorimetry [1–3]. Through measuring two emission intensities at different wavelengths and calculating their ratio, a ratiometric fluorescence system offers increased signal-to-noise ratio and improved quantification. To date, diverse ratiometric fluorescent probes have been developed based on small organic molecule [1,2] or fluorescent nanomaterials [3–10]. Organic molecule-based ratiometric fluorescent probes are usually constructed by means of internal charge transfer [11,12], excited-state intramolecular proton transfer [13,14], fluorescence resonance energy transfer [15,16] or through-bond energy transfer [17,18] mechanism, but they encounter the problem of sophisticated design and cumbersome synthesis. Fluorescent nanomaterials, including semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), and metal nanoclusters (NCs), have been proved to be ideal probes for analytical sensing and optical imaging and extensively used to construct ratiometric sensors, because of their distinct properties such as broad excitation band, narrow emission band, low photobleaching, good water solubility and relatively easy synthesis [3–10]. To obtain two emission bands, two fluorophores, either fluorescent nanomaterials and dyes or two fluorescent nanomaterials, are generally hybridized by embedding, assembling or conjugating. Various hybrid strategies such as two different QDs, CDs-QDs, dye-CDs, dye-gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), QDs-AuNCs and CDs-AuNCs, have been developed recently [19–26]. Though achieving good performance, these dual-fluorophore strategies increase the complexity of preparation. Therefore, in this sense, sole fluorophore which has intrinsic dual emissions is more intriguing for ratiometric sensing.

Intrinsic dual-emission from single nanoparticle has been found in Mn-doped ZnS (or ZnSe) QDs [3]. Based on the intrinsic dual-emitting Mn-doped QDs, several ratiometric fluorescence methods were facilely carried out for detection of temperature, glucose, diethylphosphorothioate and curcumin, respectively, without the extra assistant fluorophores [27–30]. On the other hand, althrough AuNCs have also been widely used for both single-emitting and ratiometric fluorescence sensing [22–26,31–37], intrinsic dual-emitting AuNCs have seldom been reported, except that Zheng and co-workers pioneeringly reported the glutathione (GSH) coated fluorescent gold nanoparticles with two intrinsic size-independent emissions and their ratiometric pH response [38]. The application of intrinsic dual-emitting AuNCs in analytical sensing remains unexplored.

Herein, we present a facile ratiometric fluorescence assay based on intrinsic dual-emitting AuNCs (DE-AuNCs) without auxiliary fluorophore for detection of mercuric ions (Hg2+). The GSH protected AuNCs were easily synthesized by controlling the proportion of GSH and chloroauric acid, which possessed two distinct intrinsic emission bands peaked at 610 nm and 810 nm respectively, similar to that of Zheng’s work [38]. These two different emissions were dependent on the GSH surface coverage, where the high GSH surface coverage led to the 610 nm emission and the low GSH surface coverage resulted in the 810 nm emission [38]. Upon the addition of Hg2+, the 610 nm emission was quenched while the 810 nm emission almost kept unchanged because the combination of Hg2+ with GSH in high surface coverage areas is preferred to low surface coverage areas. Thus, highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ was accomplished by the DE-AuNCs without troublesome hybridization.

Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (HAuCl4), l-glutathione (L-GSH) and all other chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents and solvents were analytical-reagent grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used throughout all the experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) with excitation and emission slits width of 10 nm. UV–vis absorption studies were carried out on a Lambda 35 spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). FT-IR spectra were collected by an Equnox 55 infrared spectrometer (Brucker, Germany). The morphology and size dimension of DE-AuNCs were revealed by a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Japan) operated at 200 kV. Prior to TEM analysis, the sample was prepared by dropping the DE-AuNCs aqueous solution on a carbon-coated Cu grid, and then dried under ambient conditions.

DE-AuNCs were synthesized according to the previous literature with minor adjustment [38]. Briefly, 5 mL of 25 mM HAuCl4 was added into 50 mL of 2.0 mM l-GSH solution under vigorous stirring. DE-AuNCs were obtained after the mixture was heated in a 95 ℃ oil bath for 2 h. The single-emitting AuNCs of 610 nm were prepared when using 50 mL of 2.4 mM l-GSH, and the single-emitting AuNCs of 810 nm were obtained when using 50 mL of 1.6 mM l-GSH. The synthesized AuNCs were purified by centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 5 min to remove large particles. Finally, the supernatant was further purified with a 0.22 μm filter membrane and collected for further application.

For detection of Hg2+, 0.3 mL DE-AuNCs and various concentrations of Hg2+ solution were transferred successively into a test tube. The mixture was diluted to 4 mL by using phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 20 mM, pH 8.0) and stirred. The solution was left to react at room temperature for 6 min. The fluorescence quenching spectra were then recorded. Both slit widths of excitation and emission are 10 nm.

To evaluate the selectivity of the measurement, experiments were conducted in the presence of different cations, including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, As3+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Co2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ag+ and Pd2+. A 5 × 10−5 M concentration of each ion was used for the selectivity test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg2+ sensing in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Real water samples were collected from distilled water, tap-water and east lake water. Prior to the fluorescence measurements, the samples were filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane to remove particulate matter. Then, the water samples were spiked with standard Hg2+ solution and analyzed by the proposed method.

The as-prepared DE-AuNCs were analyzed using UV–vis, fluorescence spectroscopy, FT-IR and HRTEM, as shown in Fig. 1 . The absorption spectrum of the DE-AuNCs displayed strong absorption in the UV region with a shoulder around 400 nm (Fig. 1a, left). The DE-AuNCs showed two distinct emission bands peaked at 610 nm and 810 nm respectively under the excitation of 440 nm (Fig. 1a, right). The 610 nm emission was owing to high GSH surface coverage on the surface of the DE-AuNCs, and the 810 nm emission was ascribed to low GSH surface coverage, which have been proved by Zheng’s work [38]. The quantum yield of the DE-AuNCs was measured to be 2.69% by the reference method, using quinine sulfate (54.6% in a 0.05 M H2SO4 solution at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm) as a standard substance. FT-IR spectroscopy demonstrated that the DE-AuNCs was coated by GSH (Fig. 1b). The typical peaks of GSH at 1409 cm−1, 1536 cm−1 and 1655 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of COOˉ and the asymmetric stretching vibration of amide II and COOˉ were found in the IR spectrum of the DE-AuNCs, while the stretching vibration of SH at 2527 cm−1 disappeared due to the formation of Au-S bonds. The HRTEM image indicated that the DE-AuNCs were well-dispersed (Fig. 1c), and the size distribution was in the range of 1–3 nm with an average diameter of 1.53 nm. The results evidenced that the GSH-coated AuNCs with intrinsic dual-emission were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence response of DE-AuNCs to Hg2+ and the mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the analytical application of the DE-AuNCs, their fluorescence response to Hg2+ was investigated. The interesting phenomenon was found that when exposed to Hg2+, the 610 nm emission of the DE-AuNCs was dramatically quenched while the 810 nm emission almost kept unchanged (Fig. 2 a), showing a ratiometric response. In order to understand the mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescence response, circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy was carried out. While free l-GSH and the single-emitting AuNCs of 810 nm showed no significant peak at 250 nm–500 nm, the single-emitting AuNCs of 610 nm gave a negative peak at 340 nm and a positive peak at 400 nm (Fig. 2b), indicating the high ordered and chiral conformation of GSH on the AuNCs due to the high GSH surface coverage. Moreover, the as-prepared DE-AuNCs gave two negative peaks at 285 nm and 428 nm and a positive peak at 360 nm (Fig. 2b), suggesting two different conformations and surface coverage of Au-S bond on the DE-AuNCs, which led to dual emissions in one single nanoparticle. All the results are consistent with the previous report [38]. We suppose that due to the extremely high affinity of thiolates toward Hg2+ [31,39], Hg2+ preferred combining with GSH in high surface coverage areas to low surface coverage areas, breaking the Au-S bond and removing GSH from the surface of the DE-AuNCs. The CD result confirmed the proposed mechanism. Upon the addition of Hg2+, both negative peak and positive peak of the DE-AuNCs reduced, implying the extent of chiral conformation declined (Fig. 2b). As a result, the high GSH surface coverage decreased and the 610 nm emission was quenched. The proposed sensing mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 2c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the ratiometric sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve better analytical performance, optimizations of pH and reaction time of the ratiometric measurement were conducted, where the 610 nm emission serves as a reporter signal while the 810 nm emission serves as a reference signal, and the value of I 610/I 810 is the ratio of the fluorescence intensities at 610 nm and 810 nm.

The as-prepared DE-AuNCs were responsive to the acidity of the medium. As seen in Fig. 3 a, when the pH value which was adjusted with PBS buffer solution increased, the emission intensity at 610 nm greatly increased while it decreased at 810 nm. This phenomenon was ascribed to the energy transfer between the emission centers of 610 nm and 810 nm in a single particle, which had been demonstrated in detail in Zheng’s work [38]. To gain high detecting sensitivity, pH 8.0 PBS buffer solution was used for subsequent experiments.

The reaction time was further optimized for rapid sensing. As shown in Fig. 3b, in the absence of Hg2+, the ratio of I 610/I 810 was relatively stable in the range of 0–60 min, suggesting that the sensor was stable and could work effectively. However, in the presence of Hg2+ (200 ppb), the ratio of I 610/I 810 decreased drastically and kept constant after 6 min. Consequently, the measurements were carried out after 6 min in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the ratiometric Hg2+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions, the sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescence system for sensing Hg2+ was explored. With the addition of increasing concentrations of Hg2+, there was a gradual quenching of the 610 nm emission, while the 810 nm emission almost remained unchanged (Fig. 4 a). The ratio of I 610/I 810 showed good linearity with Hg2+ concentration in the range of 6.25–300 ppb (Fig. 4b), giving a correlation coefficient of 0.998 (R 2 = 0.996). The limit of detection (LOD) calculated as 3 times the standard deviation of the blank divided by the slope of the standard curve was 0.19 ppb, which is quite lower than the WHO drinking-water guideline for inorganic mercury (6 μg/L) [40]. As listed in Table 1 , the LOD value of the proposed ratiometric fluorescence assay is comparable with and even lower than those of other reported methods, showing a high sensitivity toward Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the DE-AuNCs for Hg2+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the ratiometric sensing system, the effect of various ions on the ratiometric signal of DE-AuNCs was examined under the same experimental conditions. Different ions including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, As3+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Co2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ni2+ (5 × 10−5 M), and Cu2+, Ag+, Pd2+, Hg2+ (5 × 10−6 M) were added into the probe solution, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 5 a, while all other ions had almost no interference on the fluorescence intensity, only Hg2+ significantly quenched the fluorescence at 610 nm due to the extremely high affinity of GSH toward Hg2+, giving a low ratiometric signal (Fig. 5b). The result demonstrates that the ratiometric probe has high selectivity for Hg2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To appraise the feasibility of the proposed method, different real water samples such as distilled water, tap-water and east lake water were taken. All samples were first filtered through a filter paper (0.45 μm) to remove any solid impurities, and then spiked with different amounts of Hg2+. The analytical results in Table 2 showed the recoveries of Hg2+ in the range of 92.9%–106.6% with RSD of around 2.3% (n = 3), and demonstrated the good potential of the DE-AuNCs for detection of Hg2+ in environmental water samples.

In summary, we have developed a novel ratiometric fluorescence method for detection of Hg2+ on the basis of intrinsic DE-AuNCs. The ratiometric sensing system was constructed just by the facilely synthesized DE-AuNCs which simultaneously emitted the fluorescence at 610 nm and 810 nm. Hg2+ could quench the 610 nm emission and had little influence on the 810 nm emission, giving a ratiometric signal. The proposed ratiometric method exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity for sensing Hg2+ with low LOD down to 0.19 ppb which is quite lower than the WHO drinking-water guideline for inorganic mercury. Our work presented here demonstrates that DE-AuNCs are promising nanomaterials for the ratiometric sensing, and more applications are expected through the modulation of DE-AuNCs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41773126) and the Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41521001) and International Science & Technology Cooperation Program of China (2014DFA20720) and the “Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities”.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yan Yang is a Master candidate at the Faculty of Material Science and Chemical Engineering, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). Her research is about the Hg ion detection using ratiometric fluorescence method.

Liqiang Lu is an Associate professor at Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). His research is focused on detection technology on the trace pollutant in the groundwater.

Xike Tian is a Professor at the Faculty of Material Science and Chemical Engineering, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). He got his Ph.D. degree from Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He was appointed as a Doctoral Tutor in Faculty of Material Science and Chemical Engineering, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan) in 2014. His current research interests include environmental nanomaterials, optical detection technology and prevention treatment research of hazardous substances in the environment.

Yong Li is a Lecturer at Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). He got his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University. And his current research interests include optical detection technology on pollutant in the environment and nanocatalysts to generate hydrogen.

Chao Yang is an Associate professor at Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). His research is focused on detection technology on pollutant in the environment.

Yulun Nie is a Professor at the Faculty of Material Science and Chemical Engineering, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). He got his Ph.D. degree from Research center of eco-environment sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Zhaoxin Zhou is an Associate professor at Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences (Wuhan). His research is focused on optical detection technology on pollutant in the environment and oilfield sewage disposal.
